Home / Products / Crop Sourcing &amp; Traceability Management System: Cropsupply.com
Tech

Crop Sourcing &amp; Traceability Management System: Cropsupply.com

Streamlining off-taking and the supply chain across the agriculture value chain

Request a demoSee pricing

For off-takers and manufacturers, one of the biggest challenges in the agricultural value chain is sourcing raw materials from smallholder farmers. It is hard to find farmers producing the quality and quantity that meets their standards, so off-takers often pay high prices to middlemen who travel to rural areas — and the crops brought in may still miss the mark.

MazaoHub offers a Crop Sourcing and Off-taking module that lets off-takers, manufacturers, and commodity traders contract directly with individual farmers or farmer groups. An off-taker can monitor production from the moment a farmer prepares their land, ensuring the crop is grown to their quality standards.

Off-takers can collectively source from cooperatives or farmer groups and manage the entire collection process — including payment to each individual farmer — through the crop collection module. They can register their own operators or use operators within the MazaoHub platform to streamline collection, and track which crops will be harvested when, where, in what quantity and quality, and who planted what.

They can access production reports for each farmer at every level — from village to national — including the fertilizers, insecticides, and other chemicals used and the expected yield. MazaoHub's traceability tool gives complete visibility from the farm up, with invoicing and contract-farming tools to facilitate purchases. Off-takers can buy from anywhere — no need to travel region to region — and communicate with cooperatives or farmers who hold the quality and quantity they need, with crop quality, production levels, and Google Maps locations shown to ease logistics planning.

Visibility into crop production

  • Crop type and variety
  • Quantity
  • Quality
  • Location

The module also helps off-takers manage crop collection, logistics, and warehousing/storage.

Benefits of the Crop Sourcing &amp; Off-taking module

  • Source high-quality crops at competitive prices.
  • Reduce the risk of supply disruptions.
  • Improve efficiency and productivity.

More features for off-takers and manufacturers

  • Market insights: price trends and demand forecasts for smarter sourcing decisions.
  • Financial services: access to financing through MazaoHub's financial-institution partners, for both off-takers and farmers.
  • Training and support: help to use the platform effectively.
The product suite

Home / Products / Farmer Excellent Centers (FECs)
Touch

Farmer Excellent Centers (FECs)

"Kliniki za Kilimo" powered by MazaoHub technology

Request a demoSee pricing

MazaoHub products are divided into Tech and Touch. The Touch side is all the on-the-ground, people-driven, physically accessible services that bring the technology to life through direct farmer interaction.

MazaoHub-owned FECs

Fully run and managed by MazaoHub with in-house trained agronomists, these act as service clinics at the village level, offering:

  • Soil testing & fertility recommendations using portable soil kits.
  • Personalized agronomy support based on season, location, and crops.
  • Farm input distribution — verified seeds, fertilizers, and more.
  • Access to equipment rentals — tractors, planters, dryers.
  • Training & demonstrations.
  • Crop marketing support.

Locations: Tanzania, expanding to Kenya, Rwanda, Uganda, and Zambia.

Partner FECs (Franchised)

Owned by local agro-dealers or businesses but certified and powered by MazaoHub's software and protocols:

  • Operate as franchise models.
  • Receive training, branding, tools, and software.
  • Sell inputs, offer agronomy services, and market crops.
  • Serve as localized agribusiness hubs.

The clinic concept travels under local names: Ivuriro Ry'Ubuhinzi in Rwanda, Kliniki ya Kilimo in Swahili, Ilé ìtọ́jú ọgbà in Yoruba (Nigeria), Asibitin Noma in Hausa, and Ụlọ ọgwụ maka ugbo (Farm Health Clinic) in Igbo.

Franchise opportunities

Franchiseable in any rural area across Africa, Asia, or Latin America. The franchisee receives:

  • Full setup support
  • Branding & marketing materials
  • Digital platform integration
  • Agronomist training
  • Seed stock & soil-kit leasing

Touch

Uber-for-Extension Officers – Field Force

Farmers request visits, agronomists respond, and MazaoHub tracks the entire interaction

Request a demoSee pricing

MazaoHub Field Force is a platform-powered network of trained, mobile agronomists and extension officers connected to farmers through the MazaoHub platform. Think of it as Uber for agronomy services — farmers request a visit, an agronomist responds, and the whole interaction is tracked in software.

Key features

  • On-demand or scheduled farm visits.
  • Guided agronomic protocols from soil prep to post-harvest.
  • Performance tracked digitally.
  • Assignable via CRM to farmers enrolled in specific zones.
  • Compensation based on verified activities and farmer satisfaction.

Impact

  • Boosts rural youth employment.
  • Standardizes extension services.
  • Ensures consistent farmer support even in remote areas.
  • Reduces reliance on underfunded public extension systems.

Touch

Crop Supply Hubs (Agiza Mazao)

Physical crop aggregation centers linked to digital crop trading via CropSupply.com

Request a demoSee pricing

Agiza Mazao Centers (Crop Supply Hubs) are physical crop aggregation centers linked to digital crop trading through CropSupply.com. They are located in local markets, warehouses, packhouses, and FEC locations.

What they do

  • Collection & sorting of produce.
  • Quality checks.
  • Digital registration & traceability.
  • Linking verified farmers to buyers — food companies, processors, and exporters.
  • Credit recovery from input loans.

These centers give off-takers a credible, monitored source of produce directly from smallholders, with guaranteed traceability.

Field Notes  /  Farm ERP

Leading Farm ERP Platforms Worldwide.

07 Dec, 2022  ·  Admin  ·  4 min read

Farm ERP platforms are gaining in popularity as they provide farmers with the ability to manage their operations more efficiently, while also helping to reduce costs and improve production. There are several different platforms available and it can be difficult to decide which is right for you. In this article we look at seven leading Farm ERP platforms — and discuss why Mazao Hub is the most uniquely designed for the African context.

The first is FarmERP. In the industry for over 20 years, it offers a comprehensive suite of tools that let farmers manage operations from crop planning to financials, inventory and production, with real-time analytics to support better decisions.

The second is FarmLogs, a cloud-based platform that lets farmers track operations from field to office, monitor crop health, and analyse expenses, income and financial performance in real time.

The third is Agrivi, designed to help farmers manage costs, sales and inventory more efficiently, with access to real-time analytics and data.

The fourth is AgSquared, built to help farmers plan and track operations while making better decisions.

The fifth is Farmigo, giving farmers the tools to manage operations from field to office with real-time analytics.

The sixth is FarmHedge, designed to help farmers manage costs, sales and inventory with real-time analytics and data.

Finally, we have Mazao Hub. This Farm ERP platform is uniquely designed for the African context. It offers a comprehensive suite of tools to manage operations from crop planning to financials, inventory and production, with real-time analytics and data — plus a distinctive focus on sustainability and environmental awareness, making it the perfect platform for African farmers looking to become more sustainable.

Share

Field Notes  /  Climate

Is Africa Safe from Climatic Challenges?

07 Dec, 2022  ·  Admin

Climate change is one of the most pressing issues of our time. For Africa, it presents a particularly daunting challenge. As a continent already facing extreme poverty and other economic, social and political issues, climate change adds an additional layer of complexity to the region's development.

The effects of climate change in Africa are already being felt. Higher temperatures, more extreme weather events, and a greater prevalence of water-borne diseases are all predicted to increase in the coming years. These changes will have profound impacts on African countries and communities.

The agricultural sector is one of the most vulnerable to the effects of climate change. In many parts of Africa, agriculture is the main source of income, and increased droughts, floods and pests will reduce crop yields and the amount of food produced. This could lead to widespread hunger and malnutrition.

Climate change will also have a significant impact on water supplies. As temperatures rise, evaporation from surface water sources will increase, resulting in less water available for drinking, irrigation and other uses. This could have serious implications for food security and public health.

The effects of climate change will also be felt in the energy sector. Increasing temperatures will reduce the efficiency of power plants, resulting in higher energy costs for consumers. This will make it even more difficult for African countries to achieve their development goals.

African countries must take urgent action to mitigate the effects of climate change. This includes investing in renewable energy sources, improving agricultural practices, and reducing deforestation and land degradation. The region also needs to invest in adaptation strategies, such as building infrastructure and adopting more resilient farming methods.

Mazao Hub Farm ERP and Digital Agriculture platform is the solution to the effects of climate change in food production. The platform enables farmers to access real-time data and advanced analytics to help them better understand the impact of climate change on their farms. It also helps them to develop more sustainable agricultural practices that can help them increase their yields and reduce their risk of crop failure.

Mazao Hub also provides farmers with access to a range of resources, including climate forecasts, crop guides, and farm management tools. By equipping farmers with the knowledge and resources they need to adapt to a changing climate, Mazao Hub is helping to ensure that African communities are better able to manage the effects of climate change and secure their food security.

Share

Field Notes  /  Field Notes

Join Map My Farmer Campaign Using Mazao Hub App

07 Dec, 2022  ·  Admin

In recent years, African farmers have faced numerous challenges due to the changing climate, natural disasters, and economic instability. In order to maximize productivity, it is important to map all African smallholder farmers and monitor their progress, farm operations and real insights.

Why Map My Farmer Campaign?

Mapping African farmers and their operations provides a number of benefits. By understanding the unique challenges each farmer faces, policymakers and organizations can better target the right solutions. This helps to ensure that resources are used more effectively, allowing African farmers to increase their productivity and profitability.

Mapping African farmers also allows for better monitoring of progress and performance of each farmer using the Mazao Hub Farm Management system. This allows for more effective interventions, such as providing access to finance, support, advice or more improved technology. It also allows for better data collection, which can be used to inform decisions about policies and investments.

We are thrilled to announce the launch of the Mazao Hub App and its new unique campaign, Map My Farmer, with the support from UNDP, to map all farmers in Africa. This is a great opportunity for agricultural stakeholders to join and make a difference in the lives of smallholder farmers.

Map My Farmer campaign is a unique initiative that aims to gather accurate data about smallholder farmers in order to better understand and address their needs. By using the Mazao Hub App, users can easily map farmers' location, farm size, soil type and collect data about their current situation and needs. Mazao Hub goes beyond with a tool to help farmers or agronomists manage end-to-end farm operations.

Tools integrated with Mazao Hub to Map Farmers in Africa

There are a number of tools available to map African farmers using the Mazao Hub App. These include satellite imagery, aerial photography, field surveys, and google coordinates. All of these tools can be used to collect data about farmers, their operations, and the environment in which they work.

Satellite imagery is particularly useful as it provides a bird's eye view of the land — identifying land cover, changes in crop patterns, and the location of farms and fields. Aerial photography provides detailed images that reveal crop patterns and irrigation systems. Field surveys gather information on farming practices, production levels, and challenges faced. Mobile phone data, increasingly available in rural areas, helps identify patterns of movement and land use.

Field Notes  /  Story

Save My Grandmother Story: The Story of Mazao Hub

11 Dec, 2022  ·  Admin

The story of Mazao Hub is a story of pain and suffering. It is a story of my grandmother, who could not escape the exploitative buying from middlemen and post-harvest loss. It is a story of my own struggles, trial and error just to survive as a smallholder farmer, to transport farm produce to the market, a task that was filled with overwhelming feelings of inadequacy and hopelessness. It is a story of the relentless determination to fight against all odds, to create an e-commerce platform and to do better, only to fail. But it is also a story of hope, of my friend and I finding the courage to go back to the village and learn from our mistakes, to develop an innovative solution that would help smallholder farmers and transform agriculture for the better.

The story of Mazao Hub began long ago in the villages of Sumbawanga, Tanzania, where my grandmother and I worked as poor smallholder farmers. We faced issues like exploitative buying from middlemen, poor productivity, post-harvest loss, lack of direct markets to trade, logistical inefficiency, lack of credit facilities, and lack of agronomic support — everything was trial and error.

Sometimes, I had to use a bicycle to transport farm produce to the market, which was more than 30 miles away. This meant that we often lost money, time, and produce due to multiple layers of brokers involved in getting goods to the market, each of whom took a cut that ate into our family's income.

My grandmother's words to me — "When you grow up, come back and save us, we poor smallholder farmers" — stuck with me. While others may have been ashamed of or frustrated by family background, I saw opportunities. My late grandmother's voice rings in my ears every day.

I had a dream to make things better for her and all smallholder farmers even before her departure; it pains me that I failed with my first attempt and she died. My first attempt, I started an e-commerce platform for smallholder farmers called Sokoworld.com with my good friend, Adelard Urassa, as CTO, hoping to solve her problems and those of all smallholder farmers in Africa. Though our users numbered more than 20,000, we ultimately failed. We thought we were going to make things better by connecting them directly to the market, bypassing middlemen, but ended up with the same middlemen as users — not our targeted farmers.

I left the city with a heavy heart and went back to my village, desperate to learn from my mistakes and understand why my startup had failed. Working with smallholder farmers, agronomists and agro-input dealers in the village brought home the harsh reality: small-scale farmers in Africa are not getting the support they need due to a flawed local agronomy and agro-dealer distribution system and an unfriendly agricultural value chain, which leads to low productivity, poor quality produce and food wastage. Furthermore, there is no proper food system to track quality and data about smallholder farmers. Most of the data about farmers in Africa is based on guesses and inaccurate estimations, making the situation even worse. Returning to my grandmother's farm to learn from my mistakes was a bittersweet experience.

Share

Field Notes  /  Traceability

Food Traceability in Africa Is a Barrier to Trade

13 Dec, 2022  ·  Admin

In Africa, the importance of traceability in agriculture has become increasingly vital in ensuring the safety and quality of food products. As consumers demand proof of where their food comes from, traceability allows for the tracking of products throughout the supply chain. This not only assures consumers of the safety and quality of their food, but also helps to prevent product recalls and reduce business risks in the food industry.

However, for small-scale farmers in Africa, the requirements for traceability and transparency can be a barrier to trade. The market for safe and traceable food can exclude these farmers who may lack the resources to comply with strict standards.

That is where Mazao Hub comes in. Mazao Hub promotes best production practices for over 100 crops, with detailed record-keeping and monitoring of environmental and supply chain variables. This allows for the traceability and transparency of farm production, enabling small-scale farmers to be competitive in the global marketplace.

Traceability not only benefits consumers and businesses, but also empowers small-scale farmers in Africa to have a voice in the food industry. It is a crucial tool in ensuring the safety and quality of food products, while also supporting and promoting sustainable agriculture in Africa.

Overall, agriculture traceability is essential for meeting consumer demands and ensuring the safety and quality of food products. In Africa, where small-scale farmers may lack the resources to comply with strict standards, traceability solutions like Mazao Hub can help these farmers remain competitive in the global marketplace. As consumers continue to demand transparency and accountability in their food, agriculture traceability will only become more important in the future.

Share

Field Notes  /  Field Notes

Big Players Met in Musoma to Decide the Destiny of the Cotton Industry in Tanzania

14 Dec, 2022  ·  Admin

The air was thick with anticipation as the Regional Commissioners of Tanzania's seven regions gathered in the city of Musoma, along with members of parliament, political party leaders, and all other key players in the cotton industry. The meeting, held on December 13, 2022, was a crucial moment for the future of cotton production in the country.

In his emotional opening remarks, the Regional Commissioner of the Mara region, Honorable Suleiman Mzee, emphasized the significance of the meeting for the development of the cotton industry. Mzee spoke passionately about the 235 basic cooperatives in the Mara region that are engaged in the collection and sale of cotton, as well as the two national cooperatives that support the industry.

Mzee also highlighted the increasing use of digital technology in agriculture, which has improved the efficiency and accuracy of data for planning and budgeting in the sector. The Commissioner praised the Development Committee of Cooperatives and all other institutions that organized the meeting, calling it a crucial step in ensuring the sustainability of the cotton harvest and providing education to farmers and cooperative leaders on best practices.

Other regional commissioners in attendance were from the regions of Simiyu, Geita, Mwanza, Shinyanga, Tabora, Mara, and Katavi. These leaders agreed that the meeting was essential in discussing and planning ways to improve the production and market of cotton in the country. Other participants included members of parliament, district commissioners, the Director General of the Tanzania Cooperative Development Commission, the Director General of the Cotton Board, and the Director General of the Warehouse Receipt Regulatory Board.

What I believe as a stakeholder of the cotton industry

As a stakeholder of the cotton industry, I believe that the commissioners and other stakeholders who gathered at the recent meeting in Musoma should seriously consider using a new farm management software called Mazao Hub.

The software has the potential to revolutionize the way cotton is produced and marketed in Tanzania, and it could play a crucial role in helping cooperatives, the Cotton Board, the Cooperative Development Commission, and the Warehouse Receipt Regulatory Board to map cotton farmers, manage cotton productivity, and provide real-time data insights for planning and budgeting, cotton collection and warehouse management. Thanks to 6 off-takers of cotton from Shinyanga who are currently using Mazao Hub as a cotton collection management system, i.e. Gaki Investment.

One of the key benefits of using Mazao Hub is that it allows for traceability from the farm to the market.

Share

Field Notes  /  Cooperatives

Open Letter to Mr. Abdulmajid Nsekela, TCDC & Cooperatives

15 Dec, 2022  ·  Admin

Open Letter to Mr. Abdulmajid Nsekela, TCDC, Warehouse Receipt Regulatory Board, Cooperatives, Farmers' unions, investment firms, banks, insurance and various agricultural stakeholders.

A few years ago, when we started our e-commerce platform that connects smallholder farmers directly to the market, bypassing middlemen, we were filled with hope. Over 20,000 users registered as farmers, and we had more than 1,000 buyers, both local and international. However, our dreams were shattered when we discovered that more than 90% of those who claimed to be farmers were actually middlemen. Our buyers were unhappy, and deals fell through because some of the middlemen were unreliable. The data we collected was messy and unreliable. It pains me to see other platforms making the same mistakes.

Did we give up? No. We went to the village to work directly with smallholder farmers, and now we are back with a stronger, more disruptive digital agriculture and farm ERP platform. In just 10 months, we have managed to map more than 30,000 real farmers in partnership with a few farm associations and manufacturers using our new traceability farm management platform.

This open letter is based on our experience, the lessons we have learned, and the technology we have discovered that has the potential to revolutionize the food system through collaboration with cooperatives, agricultural bodies, institutions, insurance companies, agro-dealers, and all other stakeholders in the agriculture industry.

Our wake-up call came when we deployed a crop collection and distribution management system to 19 manufacturing companies. As part of our new farm ERP system, the crop collection management system enables manufacturers to track the movement of crops from the farms to collection centers and warehouses. We were able to digitize the entire process, including the payment systems. I told my co-founder: what if we digitize the entire agri chain working in partnership with key players in the agri chain?

Our journey began by working with farm cooperatives and their Agricultural Marketing Cooperative Societies (AMCOS). We realized that a revolution in agriculture is impossible without digitizing the workflow of these groups and transforming them into commercial agricultural enterprises that support their farmers. Some of the key players we work with include cooperatives, AMCOS, agronomists, agro-dealers, manufacturers and warehouses.

When we heard about Mr. Abdulmajid Nsekela being appointed as the Chairman of the Tanzania Cooperative Development Commission (TCDC) by the President, we were thrilled. It was as if our prayers had been answered. Together, we can build a giant farm ERP, e-commerce, and traceability platform that is even more advanced than Alibaba. We can serve farmers and protect their interests by partnering with officials from farmers' unions, empowering union market officials to become professional marketers who use technology to access domestic and international markets.

Share

Field Notes  /  Field Notes

The Miracle Sugarcane Farm

19 Dec, 2022  ·  Admin

Years ago, we owned a sugarcane farm. This farm was located in Lwiche, Sumbawanga. If you ask old sugarcane road traders in Sumbawanga, they know the history of the Lwiche sugarcane farm. This farm was everything to our family. Why do I say this farm was everything to our family? Without this farm, I don't think I would have been able to study until where I am now. Without this farm, even food would not have been possible. Let me explain so we understand each other.

Small-scale farmers, including myself, lived a life where our main crop was maize. We grew maize for consumption, selling a small portion to buy various daily necessities, and leaving another portion as seeds for the next season. That life, I cannot forget. During the maize planting season, there were farmers who didn't have enough maize reserves for consumption; either they sold all their maize and finished their stock, or they consumed it all before the next harvesting season. If you made a mistake and finished before the next harvest, then your family would suffer greatly from hunger.

So, how does the sugarcane farm fit into this? The sugarcane farm was our savior. There were times when our maize reserves were depleted, and the only lifeline was the sugarcane farm. During the maize season, we got maize, and during the off-season, the sugarcane farm was the source of income for the family. We harvested sugarcane and sold it to roadside vendors who came to buy at our farm. When we harvested sugarcane and sold it, we got money to buy maize for consumption. That's why we call our sugarcane farm The Miracle Sugarcane Farm.

I remember until today, I used to go to the farm with my late mother. We would meet roadside vendors crowded in the farm, each with an order for sugarcane they wanted. One wanted 10 sugarcane, another 20, another 50 and so on. My mother was the one who set the price for one sugarcane based on its size, for wholesale. My mother negotiated the price with these traders, while I acted as an accountant. I recorded daily sugarcane sales, counted money, and hid it in my clothes. Have I told you, this is the reason I studied accounting? The money earned from the sugarcane farm was used to pay for my brothers' boarding school fees.

Our farming life was a lonely life. We had no close agronomic support. Yes, in our village, we had an agricultural officer, but he was of no help. We never saw him in the fields, nor did we receive any assistance from him. So, we relied on ourselves. Our harvests were poor. Our neighbors, who were farmers like us, were as poor as us. Even now, the lives of many smallholder farmers are the same. Even after my mother passed away, we continued to farm with difficulty under the supervision of my grandmother, whom we called Grandma Ndango. Various NGOs came, took our information, and promised to help us but never returned.

The sugarcane farm is what enabled me to finish university. Thanks to the government's education loan, if not for that, maybe I wouldn't have gone to university because I couldn't afford it. As I graduated and returned home to my dear village, I paid a visit to our beloved sugarcane farm. It shatters my soul to witness the struggles that smallholder farmers in my community continue to face even today.

I started the company MazaoHub to bring precision agriculture technologies to small-scale farmers because of the problems I encountered as a small-scale farmer. Before MazaoHub, I started an agritech company that was like an e-commerce platform connecting farmers. I failed miserably. Do you think I gave up? My failure pushed me to go back to my village, and I visited our sugarcane farm. I remember crying in the sugarcane farm alone and telling that farm, "you are the one that pushed me to start the agritech company that failed. Tell me where I'm stuck? Give me strength to start afresh." I had no idea what happened, but that Miracle Sugarcane Farm gave me a new inspiration that led to drawing a new map of a new agritech — the birthplace of MazaoHub Precision Agriculture.

African farmers have been failed by inefficient agricultural extension delivery services. Agricultural extension delivery is disorganized, not data-driven, and that's why farmers are not productive, not connected to market and not creditworthy. Imagine an extension officer overseeing more than 3,000 farmers manually. Many farmers are not served, and nobody cares.

MazaoHub solves these problems. We recognize that if agricultural extension delivery is streamlined, many farmers will be served, and we will achieve high productivity. To achieve true agricultural productivity, farmers need a comprehensive approach that includes geospatial data about their land, historical and predictive climate information, soil nutrient testing, crop suitability analysis, budgeting based on current market prices, monitoring tools to track crop health, guidance on appropriate local fertilizers and herbicides, understanding of market trends, and proper post-harvest practices. All these services must be onboarded together, and we use AI to provide farmers with personalized real-time prescriptions, recommendations, and insights — just like how doctors provide prescriptions in the health sector. To deliver these services, we cannot rely on extension officers without software tools, nor on software alone. That's why MazaoHub delivers these services through software and on-the-ground support, with a simple, local app that functions even with low connectivity or offline.

Share

Field Notes  /  Climate

Climatic Change and Food Crisis in Africa — Who Will Solve It?

20 Dec, 2022  ·  Admin

Is it not heart-wrenching that the crisis of food in Africa is not unfamiliar to us? In the mid-80s, a devastating one million lives were lost due to food shortages in Ethiopia. The Sahel region also experienced a food crisis in the 1970s and 1980s due to drought. In the 1990s, Somalia was struck by widespread famine. Even in the current century, East African countries have struggled with persistent food insecurity, with 22 million people currently in danger of starvation. Despite all the technological progress and innovations available, is it not unacceptable that we are still grappling with the same issues as the mid-80s?

Climate change may not be the sole cause of the devastating famines that have plagued Africa for decades, but it is certainly a significant contributor. Poor agricultural conditions, such as adverse weather, insect infestations, and improper land use, have been at the root of these hunger crises. However, when combined with geopolitical instability, the result is a recipe for disaster.

While climate change may not be the root cause of Africa's regional famines, it is certainly not innocent in the matter. Much like an earthquake that continues to devastate a region, climate change exacerbates already weak foundations, making it even more difficult to rebuild and fortify while the ground is constantly shifting. It amplifies the challenges and struggles that communities in Africa are already facing.

The devastating issue of food insecurity in sub-Saharan Africa will only continue to worsen unless we deeply comprehend the fundamental causes. While it may be tempting to solely blame climate change, it is only one of several factors. In order to effectively address this issue, we must also acknowledge and confront our flawed policies and disorganized food systems.

Importing food is one of the most fragile and unreliable foundations. Take East African countries such as Ethiopia, Somalia, and Kenya. These countries are currently experiencing their third drought in the past decade. This drought has been so intense that it has caused an unbearable famine claiming the lives of one person every 48 seconds, according to estimates by Oxfam and Save the Children. The problem has been exacerbated by irregular and unaffordable food imports from Ukraine and Russia. The Russian-Ukrainian war and global inflation have caused the price of wheat in Africa to increase by over 45%, as noted by the African Development Bank.

Furthermore, the desperate shortage of high-quality agricultural data in sub-Saharan Africa is a deeply frustrating and complex issue. It is crucial to have data on areas vulnerable to climate change and on farmers who may be affected by it. It is also essential to have all farmers and their farmlands mapped, with information on their primary crops, real-time crop production, irrigation schemes, restricted areas, the climatic conditions of every area, the necessary cultivation density of each area, GAP data, and areas in need of attention in order to plan for economic growth and efforts to alleviate poverty. Tell me, which African country has all of this data and can prove me wrong?

The United Nations has announced that the average amount invested annually in agriculture by low- and lower-middle-income countries is a staggering figure. If agriculture accounts for more than 25% of GDP in some developing countries, employs 63% of the world's impoverished population, and has the potential to improve food security for 80% of them, then why has this investment not resulted in stronger economies and improved livelihoods?

Climate change reveals the already existing vulnerabilities in crucial systems such as roads, bridges, train tracks, and ports.

Share

Field Notes  /  Field Notes

The Shocking Struggles of Africa's Smallholder Farmers — and How We Can Help

05 Jan, 2023  ·  Admin

"Simply type in Tshs. 18,000,000, I am aware of how to plead for this loan."

The pleading voice of my neighbor, a smallholder farmer in Katumba, Sumbawanga, reached me. I was assisting her in creating a budget to obtain a loan from a bank. I inquired: "Have they ever visited your farm? Are they aware of its size? Do they understand the climate conditions of your farm? Have you ever produced any farm income report in the past to gain their trust for a loan without collateral? Will that Tshs. 18 million be given to you in cash or will they pay input suppliers so you can obtain the inputs you need? Is Tshs. 18 million sufficient?"

She replied with sadness:

"Too many questions that I don't have answers for. Every season I try to put together this budget and proposal. I know I won't get this loan. Sometimes it feels like being a smallholder farmer is a curse. Everyone seems to care about us, but they really don't. Obtaining a loan from a bank as a smallholder farmer? We are a joke to them. I'm just going through the motions, hoping maybe a miracle will happen. Please just help me finish the budget."

As a child of smallholder farmers, I have personally witnessed the struggles and hardships that these brave men and women face on a daily basis. Growing up with my beloved grandmother, who was also a smallholder farmer, she repeatedly warned me: "When you go to college and become educated, do not become like those so-called intellectuals who come and interrogate us as if they are judges, pretending to want to help. They take our pictures and videos and then vanish." Her words have stayed with me, driving me to be a truly empathetic and supportive ally to these resilient and hardworking individuals.

Drawing from my personal experience as a smallholder farmer, I want to share the challenges that smallholder farmers like myself have encountered and continue to face today.

1. Access to credit and insurance

Perhaps the biggest challenge we faced — and still face — is the lack of access to credit. Without access to loans or other forms of financial assistance, it is difficult to invest in things like seeds or fertilizer. As a result, farmers' crops often fail to thrive, leading to a vicious cycle of poverty and despair.

Credit is a form of financial assistance that allows us to borrow money in order to invest in our farms and improve our livelihoods. It is a crucial tool that allows us to take risks, innovate, and grow our businesses. However, smallholder farmers in Africa often struggle to access credit due to a lack of collateral, inadequate documentation and financial history, and a lack of access to financial institutions.

This lack of credit has a ripple effect on smallholder farmer communities. Without the necessary financial support, they are unable to purchase the inputs like seeds, fertilizers, and equipment that would allow them to increase their yields and improve their income. This means that they are unable to feed their families and provide for communities, leading to a cycle of poverty and malnutrition.

Share

Field Notes  /  Field Notes

Revolutionary Global Digital Agriculture Companies Transforming the Future of Farming

13 Jan, 2023  ·  Admin

In this current age of technology, where every industry is embracing the advancements of the digital world, it is imperative for decision-makers to urgently evaluate which areas must be prioritized for digital transformation in order to optimize resources and reach maximum efficiency and productivity.

Why agriculture needs immediate digital transformation

A few months ago, I had the honor of crossing paths with a highly accomplished woman in the realm of e-commerce. As I poured out my heart to her about my burning passion to revolutionize the agriculture sector in Africa, she coldly dismissed me with the words: "You are destined for failure. The agriculture industry is one of the most challenging sectors to digitize. I have attempted it on multiple occasions, yet each time I have been met with failure. Coordinating with rural farmers is an insurmountable task."

The unbreakable bond that unites humanity is the pulsating heartbeat of agriculture — the very sustenance that nourishes and sustains us all. Without the bountiful harvests of our farmers, we would wither and perish. Yet, despite the vital role agriculture plays, a mere handful of nations prioritize its development. The lands devoted to growing our food are often neglected and left to languish in poverty. The struggles faced by agriculture today are myriad — a dwindling availability of fertile land and fresh water, outdated cultivation techniques, the depletion of natural resources, and the scourge of environmental pollution all threaten the sustainability of our food sources.

While using tried and true farming techniques, farmers are facing a brutal business landscape, harsh market conditions, and fierce competition, more than ever before. Since the financial stakes associated with a decrease in agricultural yield are astronomical, it is imperative for farmers today to make wise agribusiness-related choices at all levels and at all stages of food production. The only way for farmers and producers to stay afloat in today's rapidly evolving digital world is to integrate cutting-edge solutions in all stages of the decision-making process of a farming operation.

Why your farm needs a provider now more than ever

The past ten years have witnessed the emergence of various agriculture solution providers, all dedicated to equipping today's farmers with every necessary tool to manage their farms and livestock, with the ultimate goal of achieving maximum productivity and minimizing wastage. The desire behind these systems is to infuse data and efficiency into the agribusiness sector, ensuring timely and precise actions. Agriculture technology companies have crafted these systems with great care, incorporating potent tools for data acquisition, processing, and reporting that are essential for managing processes in the agribusiness environment.

The most prominent providers of agricultural solutions working to revolutionize agribusiness practices include MazaoHub, SAP Agribusiness, Microsoft Dynamics, FarmLogs, and AGRIVI 360. Among these, MazaoHub stands apart as the platform purpose-built for the realities of African smallholder farming — local, affordable, and designed to work both online and offline.

Share

Popular Notes

Leading Farm ERP Platforms WorldwideWhy We Started MazaoHubSoil Is the MotherToday, I Visited My Grandmother’s Farm

Field Notes  /  Field Notes

2023 Agriculture Apocalypse: The Dire Challenges Facing Our Food Supply

13 Jan, 2023  ·  Admin

We are all connected by agriculture

If there's one thing that connects the people of the world, it's agriculture. All the world's population depends on agriculture for their daily food essentials and other basic requirements. Despite the insatiable hunger for agriculture in our daily existence, we have abandoned everything to the small-scale farmers who are disconnected, underprivileged, unsupported, and untraceable, with little understanding of how to cultivate the land sustainably.

The areas dedicated to farming and the growth of edible plants are mostly rural, underdeveloped, disconnected, unmapped, with little or no understanding of their climatic conditions. As the world's very survival rests heavily on the thriving state of the agricultural industry, it is of paramount importance that we closely monitor and address the struggles this sector faces. The most pressing challenges currently facing the agricultural industry include a sharp decline in the availability of fertile land and precious freshwater, the use of outdated farming techniques, the depletion of vital natural resources, and the alarming increase in environmental pollution.

Growing world population

The escalating rate at which the world's population is expanding is causing immense alarm, with projections indicating that it will soar from its current 7.5 billion to an overwhelming 10 billion by 2050. This means that the resources required to produce food for these burgeoning numbers must also expand at a commensurate rate. Not only will the growing population necessitate an increase in food production, but as the economic situation of developing countries improves, more individuals will be able to afford luxuries such as meat, fish, and dairy.

Climate change and global warming

The devastating effects of climate change and global warming on agriculture are one of the greatest threats facing our world today. Studies have shown a 5% decline in crop yields per decade due to the detrimental impact of climate change on agriculture. The increasing frequency of heatwaves, weather volatility, droughts, storms, insect infestations, and flooding caused by global warming pose dire threats to agriculture.

Climate change is anticipated to substantially contribute to food insecurity in the future, with food prices likely to soar as a result of reduced agricultural productivity. Increasingly unpredictable weather and catastrophic events such as floods and droughts can alter growing seasons, restrict the availability of water, and lead to an increase in the growth of weeds, pests, and fungi, all of which can significantly decrease crop productivity. Climate change forces smallholder farmers to move to more fertile lands, but as certain areas become climatically unsuitable for agriculture, the availability of fertile lands will continue to decrease.

Share

Field Notes  /  Field Notes

Feeding a Population of More Than 9 Billion People

17 Jan, 2023  ·  Admin

By 2050, the world will face a daunting challenge: feeding a population of more than 9 billion people. Africa, in particular, spends 35 billion US dollars importing food instead of creating the conditions to grow more of it. The task of feeding the fast-growing population, achieving environmentally sustainable food production, securing healthy diets, and minimizing food waste is a daunting one that we cannot leave to smallholder farmers alone. These farmers are often disconnected, underprivileged, unsupported, and untraceable, with little understanding of how to cultivate the land sustainably.

But there is hope. Mazao Hub is on a mission to change the narrative. We are a farm management and farm ERP platform that provides tools for farmers to register and map their farmlands with Google coordinates. Agronomists can have complete control over all agronomic operations, gain deep insight into all farm processes, optimize costs, and improve yields with powerful analytics.

As an ecosystem, we are empowering farmers, agronomists, cooperatives, AMCOS, agro-dealers and all other key stakeholders in agriculture with technological tools to manage, monitor, and control complex farm operations. Our traceability features enable buyers to identify, at any specified stage of the food supply chain, the production stage and the responsible farmer.

We are currently working with cooperatives, AMCOS, extension officers, agricultural boards, and ministries of agriculture in all East African countries, global buyers, manufacturers and more. Open your account today and request a demonstration. Together, we can meet the challenge of feeding the world sustainably.

Share

Field Notes  /  Field Notes

The Future of Farming: Understanding the Magic of Precision Farming

29 Jan, 2023  ·  Admin

Overview

The evolution of precision farming is taking the agriculture industry by storm, shaking its very foundations in the same way the John Deere steel plow did in the mid-1800s. With the integration of cutting-edge technologies like data collection tools, IoT, GPS, GIS, remote sensing, and satellite imagery, farmers are now equipped to make informed decisions that enhance their output and reduce their impact on the environment.

The impact of precision farming is already being felt. A recent study by the Association of Equipment Manufacturers (AEM) showed that farmers have increased their production by 4%, reduced their fertilizer use by 7%, decreased herbicide applications by 9%, lowered fossil fuel use by 6%, and gained a 4% savings in water use. This is just the tip of the iceberg.

The USDA predicts that as precision agriculture becomes more widespread, it will transform the way we grow our food — using fewer resources and becoming more sustainable. Precision farming is not only revolutionizing the industry, it's also paving the way for a more environmentally conscious future, where we can nourish the world while preserving it for future generations.

What does precision farming mean?

Precision farming, also known as site-specific crop management, blends technology with expertise to deliver the ultimate precision in crop management. With the integration of data collection, remote sensing, GPS, and GIS, farmers can precisely respond to the variability of their fields and make informed decisions to optimize production.

Gone are the days of relying on guesswork and assumptions. Precision farming allows farmers to make decisions based on actual field conditions, such as soil types and moisture levels. They can now vary the amount of seed planted or fertilizer spread with pinpoint accuracy — down to the square meter and even square centimeter of a field. This level of precision not only maximizes crop yields, it also saves time, resources, and money.

Precision farming is more than just a technology — it's a mindset, a way of doing things differently, and a way of thinking about the future. It's about embracing the power of technology to transform the way we grow our food and care for our planet.

For what purpose is precision farming employed?

Precision farming gives farmers the tools to make resource-management decisions based on real-time data collected in their fields. From optimizing the application of inputs to monitoring crop health, precision farming is being used to revolutionize the way we grow our food. The components that make it work include auto-steer, variable rate technology (VRT), remote sensing, data collection and analytics, and yield monitors.

At MazaoHub, our mission is to bring this same magic of precision farming to Africa's smallholder farmers — through soil testing, geospatial mapping, climate data and AI-driven recommendations delivered in a simple local app, backed by on-the-ground extension support.

Share

Field Notes  /  Cooperatives

MazaoHub's Quest to Empower African Cooperatives into Thriving Businesses

01 Feb, 2023  ·  Admin

Imagine a world where farmers don't have to rely on outdated methods of farm management and record-keeping. A world where cooperatives and Agricultural Marketing Companies (AMCOs) can easily access and analyze all their data in one place. This is the world that MazaoHub is working towards.

MazaoHub is a digital agriculture platform that aims to digitize and revolutionize the operations of cooperatives and their AMCOS. Our mission is to start with cooperatives and their farmers, providing them with a comprehensive farm management system that can be customized to their specific needs.

Gone are the days of struggling with scattered and incomplete records, manual data entry, and time-consuming reports. With MazaoHub, farmers and cooperatives can now easily track their farm activities, inventory, sales, and expenses. The platform offers real-time data analytics and insights, allowing users to make informed decisions and streamline their operations.

With great respect, we extend an invitation to cooperatives to join us in implementing MazaoHub. Our platform offers a range of modules to meet the diverse needs of each cooperative.

1. Farmer Profiling

We understand the importance of having accurate and up-to-date information about farmers. That's why we created a mobile app that allows cooperative extension officers to register farmers within just two minutes. The app works even without internet access and records vital information such as assets, pictures, locations, and any other requirements requested by the cooperative. By updating each farmer's information every season, MazaoHub provides financial institutions with a clear understanding of the farmer's profile, making it easier for them to provide the necessary support.

2. Field Mapping and Plant Identification

Our field mapping module uses Google coordinates to accurately map out the size of each farm. This enables cooperative extension officers or farmers to easily measure the size of their farms and identify the plants growing in each field. This stage is critical for traceability and monitoring, and allows cooperatives to keep track of the number of farmers, the number of acres each farm owns, and the number of plants owned by each farmer.

3. Agronomy Service Management

Our Agronomy Service Management module is the heart of this technology and provides even more insight into the operations of each farm. With the combination of field mapping and plant identification, cooperatives can deliver scientific, data-driven agronomy support to every member — transforming the cooperative into a thriving, commercially-minded business that truly serves its farmers.

Share

Field Notes  /  Cooperatives

Unleashing the Untapped Potential of Cooperatives

02 Mar, 2023  ·  Admin

Introduction

There is a common proverb across Africa — "umuntu ngumuntu ngabantu" in Zulu — a person is not a person without other people. This proverb has never been truer than when it comes to the untapped potential of cooperatives in Tanzania and beyond. During the Cooperative Conference with stakeholders held on 27 February 2023 at CRDB Bank Headquarters, I was fortunate enough to have a minute to give my opinion and say this phrase: "Ushirika (Cooperatives) is a big thing."

I am a young person with a strong passion for cooperatives, and whenever I read the Cooperative Society principles, I tremble and end up saying to myself: Ushirika is a big thing. These cooperatives, made up of small-scale producers, are the unsung heroes of our economy. They provide the raw materials for the biggest manufacturing companies; they produce more than 60% of the food we eat every day. The potential of cooperatives is not just theoretical; it has been proven in practice.

Cooperatives that became global powerhouses

  • In India, the Amul Dairy Cooperative has become a global brand, creating jobs for millions of people.
  • Mondragon Corporation (Spain): the largest in the world, with over 80,000 members and 260 companies, generating over €12 billion in revenue.
  • Land O'Lakes (USA): over 3,000 member-owners generating over $14 billion in annual revenue.
  • Oromia Coffee Farmers Cooperative Union (OCFCU) (Ethiopia): over 400,000 members, generating over $100 million in revenue while ensuring fair prices.
  • Groupe d'Action pour le Progrès et la Paix (GAPP) (Senegal): produces and exports organic mangoes, creating sustainable jobs.
  • GIE-PC (Mali): over 11,000 members generating over $20 million annually from cotton sales.
  • Nile River Cooperative (Egypt): over 2,000 members generating over $2 million annually from citrus.
  • NUCAFE (Uganda): over 200,000 members, instrumental in improving the quality and value of Ugandan coffee.

Why Ushirika is a big thing

Before delving further, allow me to applaud the cooperative societies that participated in the conference. I was very impressed by Karagwe District Cooperative Union Limited (KDCU Ltd) and greatly pleased with their various projects — the school project, hotel project, factory project, cooperative farms, and other initiatives. There are many cooperative societies in Tanzania that serve as examples to be emulated.

First and foremost, cooperatives are founded on the principles of democratic ownership and control, where each member has a voice. MazaoHub exists to transform these cooperatives into commercial powerhouses — digitizing their workflow, giving them data on their farmers, and connecting them directly to markets, finance and inputs.

Share

Field Notes  /  Field Notes

Exploitation or Incompetence? The Truth Behind Small-Scale Farmers and Millers' Struggles

03 Mar, 2023  ·  Admin

The life of a small-scale farmer is full of many challenges. I am left speechless when a lot of money is sent to help small-scale farmers, yet the results are very minimal. What could be the reason for this? Where do we get accurate data to closely monitor what is happening?

During my training of extension officers in rural areas on how to utilize the MazaoHub farm ERP system for effective management of farmers and end-to-end farm operations, I was taken aback and greatly concerned to observe that millers bear the responsibility of supporting farmers. Despite the inflow of funds, including grants of different kinds, it's perplexing to see millers at the forefront of providing services to farmers. This raises several questions: where is the money going, and how is it feasible for millers to undertake this role?

Did you know that these millers are not only the main lenders to smallholder farmers but also the primary market for their produce? Why have they been left with all the burden?

During my experience in rural areas, I have personally witnessed small-scale farmers relying on millers for loans to purchase inputs. After harvesting, they store their crops in the miller's warehouse and sell it back to the miller at a lower price to repay the loan. Unfortunately, the miller is often the only support system for these farmers. They provide loans, storage facilities, and even seek out markets. However, there are times when the miller purchases the crops at a low price and then sells them to a broker at an even lower price, who then sells them to someone else for a higher profit. Under these circumstances, it's doubtful that the lives of small-scale farmers would improve.

Moreover, I've also observed small-scale farmers reaching out to millers after harvesting to request that the miller pay for transportation of their crops to the warehouse. It begs the question of where the funds designated to support small-scale farmers are being utilized.

We created MazaoHub farm ERP with a dual purpose: to empower farmers to manage their own farming process until harvest, and to enable millers to manage the farmers they lend to, store their crops, and access direct markets through our partner millers and warehouses. This solution also helps to create financial records of each farmer's sales, providing them with a broad scope of borrowing opportunities and direct access to customers without going through brokers.

Share

Field Notes  /  Field Notes

Scarred But Not Broken: The Unstoppable Journey of MazaoHub Farm ERP

07 Mar, 2023  ·  Admin

Our Journey

Our journey has been a rollercoaster of emotions — from the highs of creating more than 25 direct job opportunities and 11,000 indirect employments in agriculture, to the crushing lows of watching our first company fail. It was a painful lesson to learn: that farming is not just about the land and the crops, but about the entire agriculture chain and engaging all stakeholders. The consequences of our mistakes were dire, with jobs lost and lives shattered.

But we refused to give up. Our love for the land, our passion for farming, is in our blood, our very DNA. It's our destiny, our heritage. And so we picked ourselves up, dusted off our wounds, and started again — this time, with renewed vigor and determination.

Through MazaoHub, we have expanded tenfold compared to our previous attempt. It hasn't been easy — there have been countless sleepless nights, endless struggles, and countless setbacks. But we've persevered, fueled by our unwavering belief in the power of agriculture to change lives and transform rural communities.

More than farm profiling

MazaoHub is not just a system, it's a lifeline. I know there are skeptics out there who think that MazaoHub is just another farmer registration system. MazaoHub is so much more than that. It's an integrated farm ERP and farm management system that involves all stakeholders — from rural smallholder farmers, commercial farmers and buyers to government officials and NGOs. It's a system that manages end-to-end farm operations, from planning and projections to real-time farm monitoring with data insights, to harvest and crop collection and movement.

Commercial farmers are reaping the benefits of MazaoHub and seeing real results. With 55 factories on board, MazaoHub is revolutionizing crop production by providing a comprehensive system for managing farmers and their raw materials. Exporters are leveraging MazaoHub to generate detailed traceability reports, while cooperatives are using it to effectively manage their smallholder farmers. Even agricultural boards are jumping on the MazaoHub bandwagon.

Share

Field Notes  /  Field Notes

Why Small-Scale Farmers Are Failing

26 Mar, 2023  ·  Admin

Agriculture is not just a simple task; it requires diligent monitoring and scientific analysis to improve the livelihoods of farmers, especially small-scale farmers. I understand this firsthand from our story of an old startup that failed. We created an e-commerce platform to register farmers, hoping to connect them directly to the market. However, we failed miserably, and it hurts me to see others still making the same mistakes. Our first attempt, five years ago, was an e-commerce platform for smallholder farmers called Sokoworld.com — hoping to make things better by connecting them directly to the market, bypassing middlemen, but we ended up with the same middlemen as users, not our targeted farmers. More than 20,000 users, and 80% of them middlemen claiming to be farmers.

We left the city with a heavy heart and went back to the village. Working with smallholder farmers, agronomists and agro-input dealers brought home the harsh reality: small-scale farmers in Africa are not getting the support they need due to a flawed local agronomy and agro-dealer distribution system and an unfriendly agricultural supply chain, which leads to low productivity, poor quality produce, unsustainable production and food wastage. Furthermore, there is no proper food system to track quality and data about smallholder farmers. Most of the data about farmers in Africa is based on guesses and inaccurate estimations.

Because of our mistakes and challenges, we came back stronger with a SaaS-based digital agriculture and farm ERP platform to manage farmers, farming activities and complex farm operations — making data-driven decisions, optimizing cost, and improving yields based on real-time insights and advanced field analytics from planning to harvest. It is designed for individual farmers, commercial farmers, extension officers, cooperatives, agro-dealers, manufacturers, traders and all other agricultural stakeholders.

Extension officers

It was a painful lesson, but we realized that registering farmers alone is not enough. To make a real difference in the lives of farmers, we must invest in improving the performance of all stakeholders who work with and serve farmers, including extension officers. We came back 10 times stronger because we learned that we cannot expect to increase crop production and improve the lives of farmers without first improving the performance of extension officers. The extension officer is our first partner in our innovation.

Extension officers play a vital role in managing farmers and end-to-end farm activities. But how can we empower them to manage farmers scientifically? That's where MazaoHub Farm ERP comes in. We believe that providing extension officers with an agronomy management software system can optimize their work and enable them to make data-driven decisions in real-time — helping them plan with farmers all the way from land preparation to harvest, and then monitor end-to-end farm operations.

Share

Field Notes  /  Field Notes

The Struggle for Data in Agriculture: MazaoHub's Solution

11 Apr, 2023  ·  Admin

The agricultural industry is the backbone of most economies, providing food and raw materials for various industries. However, despite its importance, it is plagued by a lack of quality data that affects all stakeholders — from farmers to input suppliers, distributors, buyers, manufacturers, cooperatives, development agencies, financial institutions, insurance and governments. This broken system has led to tragic consequences for farmers, agro-dealers and other agricultural stakeholders alike, as they are unable to secure loans or investments due to unreliable records that fail to persuade lenders and investors.

Extension officers and other MSMEs, in particular, face significant challenges as they lack proper devices and information, relying instead on traditional methods. The use of notebooks for record-keeping is common, making it difficult for extension officers to monitor their farmers' progress. Warehouse owners also face challenges in determining the value of farmers' produce.

The absence of appropriate repayment models and reluctance to lend to farmers has led to losses for insurance companies, financial institutions, and input funds. Moreover, crop off-takers, exporters, and manufacturing companies that rely on raw materials from farmers struggle to acquire the produce they require to meet their quality standards and quantities. This is because of the lack of easily accessible data about farmers — such as their location, crop type, stage of production, inputs used, harvest timing, and yields.

As a result, buyers cannot obtain traceability reports, and farmers face market challenges not because of a lack of demand, but because there is a mismatch between the quantities and quality they produce and the needs of the market. This mismatch is due to a lack of communication between farmers and buyers from the start of the farming season until harvest. Most of the products grown end up in local markets, with more than 40% going to waste as post-harvest losses.

The government is also struggling to obtain accurate and clean data on the entire agricultural supply chain — crop production from each farmer, the movement of produce until it is sold, input suppliers and their sales, crop distributors, warehouse owners and the value and quantity stored, crops transported to factories, factory production, cooperative associations, the production of each member, large and small buyers, and the total acreage cultivated or left uncultivated in a given year.

This lack of quality data has had devastating consequences. MazaoHub's solution is to digitize the entire chain — capturing clean, real-time, traceable data from every farmer and every stakeholder in one connected system, so that finance, insurance, markets and government planning can finally rest on facts rather than guesswork.

Share

Field Notes  /  Field Notes

Exploited and Impoverished: The Painful Reality of Smallholder Farmers in Africa

05 May, 2023  ·  Admin

In Africa, particularly Tanzania, based on our experience as smallholder farmers, many smallholder farmers do not view agriculture as a profitable business. Even those who do often do not operate their farms in a way that generates a profit.

For instance, a farmer may spend six months planting rice and then meet with a broker to negotiate the price of their harvest. However, they often agree to a price without considering the costs they incurred during the six months and without calculating potential profit. Consequently, after many years, these farmers remain poor, while brokers and others who buy their products become wealthy.

If one were to visit agro-input shops and encounter real farmers who do not view agriculture as a business, one would feel pity for them. The struggle of these farmers to purchase fertilizers is evident, with prices often fluctuating, and sometimes they are forced to buy in bulk, which is not necessary.

Another farmer may struggle for months to grow their vegetables or watermelons without calculating their expenses, the time they invested, or keeping a record of their costs. Consequently, they have no way to know if the time they spent waiting for the harvest equals the value they were paid for their produce.

Imagine a farmer waiting for watermelons to grow for three months, only to have brokers from the city come and harvest the watermelons, eating some and destroying others, without regard for the effort the farmer invested. The farmer pays for transportation to take the watermelons to the market but will not receive payment until they are sold, which could take weeks. Unfortunately, it is common for brokers to report that the produce is of poor quality, meaning the farmer must take a loss.

Another issue is the contract farming system, where smallholder farmers grow crops at their own expense, often with promises of support from agricultural extension officers who rarely show up. Some officers add to the farmer's expenses, and when it is time to collect the harvest, the company may report that 50% of the produce is of poor quality. If the farmer asks for the return of the low-quality produce to sell elsewhere, they are refused.

These smallholder farmers deserve better. Technology — through MazaoHub's farm ERP, cost and profit tracking, agronomy support, traceability and direct market access — gives every farmer the records, the science and the bargaining power to finally treat farming as the profitable business it should be.

Share

Field Notes  /  Funding

The Harsh Reality: How African Agriculture Fails Small-Scale Farmers Despite Investments and Grants

31 May, 2023  ·  Admin

Introduction

In the vast lands of Africa — in Tanzania, where the sun shines fiercely on fertile soil — a tale of determination and innovation unfolds. It is the story of visionary individuals who refuse to yield to the challenges that plague the agricultural sector. We will not whine. We will not complain. Complaining is not an option. Instead, we will conquer. We will succeed. These were the resounding words that echoed within the minds of UjuziNet's co-founders, Adelard Urassa and myself. We understood that many venture capitalists still harbor skepticism when it comes to investing in the agricultural sector compared to fintech and other sectors.

Many have encountered us. Each time, we demonstrate the MazaoHub Farm ERP System, our achievements, the number of farmers we have reached without external investments, the industries utilizing the system, the off-takers, agro-dealers, cooperatives, and all stakeholders. Their main questions are always: How did you come up with this idea? How long did it take to develop this system? How many investors do you have? Which countries is this system from?

There are others who, upon seeing us, underestimate us and view us as just another startup seeking aid or grants. But later, when they witness how we carry out our work, our strategies — predominantly from our own initiative — they ask themselves how we can do it.

Many assume we started today, but the truth is that we have faced numerous failures. Our journey began over three years ago. We started by developing an e-commerce platform to connect farmers directly with markets, bypassing middlemen. We failed. We acquired over 20,000 users, thinking they were farmers, only to discover they were middlemen. Our big mistake was thinking that the transformation in agriculture begins at the market level, but the challenges of agriculture are vast — production, aggregation, finance, insurance, distribution, processing, markets, and much more. We learned a great deal and realized that agriculture requires its own farm ERP system.

The Birth of UjuziNet

The UjuziNet Group emerged as a technology company with a singular mission — to develop intelligent applications and ERP systems that automate, simplify, and manage core business operations and processes. Our diverse portfolio of solutions, including EMASUITE, ADBOX AI, MAZAOHUB, and UJUZINET, took center stage, with our flagship product, MazaoHub, leading the way.

But before reaching this level, I called my friend and co-founder. I told him that if we truly want to revolutionize agriculture, we must ensure that we can sustain ourselves — that we can pay all our bills without relying on anyone.

Share

Field Notes  /  Inputs

The Silent Ecological Time Bomb: Ignoring Fertilizer Recommendations

01 Jun, 2023  ·  Admin

Introduction

In 2015, the report titled "A Soiled Reputation" shed light on the adverse impacts of mineral fertilizers in tropical agriculture. This publication sparked a heated debate between proponents of organic fertilizers and those who advocate for mineral fertilizers. However, the essence lies not in the fertilizers themselves but in their application. Similar to food, there is nothing inherently wrong with fertilizers; it is the improper use and application that can lead to detrimental effects.

Imagine you enjoy eating meat. Despite the fact that meat contains a lot of protein, you consume a large amount of it without any other food. Or perhaps you enjoy eating a lot of ugali, especially in Africa, to the point of feeling bloated. Will your body be truly healthy? Food must be balanced — just like with fertilizers. Understanding the significance of fertilizer application, and preferably utilizing a combination of mineral and organic sources, can greatly enhance crop growth and increase food production.

The potential pitfalls of improper fertilizer application

Improper application of mineral fertilizers has been associated with several negative consequences. When not used judiciously, these fertilizers can lead to environmental pollution, soil degradation, and reduced crop yields. Excessive use of nitrogen-based fertilizers can result in nitrogen leaching, contaminating water bodies and contributing to harmful algal blooms. Phosphorus runoff from fields can cause eutrophication in nearby water systems, leading to oxygen depletion and aquatic ecosystem imbalances. Indiscriminate application of potassium fertilizers can disrupt the balance of essential nutrients in the soil, affecting plant health and overall productivity.

Optimizing fertilizer application

To harness the potential benefits of fertilizers and minimize their negative impacts, it is crucial to focus on proper application techniques. Several factors must be considered, including soil type, crop nutrient requirements, and environmental conditions. Soil testing is an essential first step to determine existing nutrient levels and to formulate an appropriate fertilization plan. This allows farmers to tailor the application to the specific needs of their crops, reducing wastage and potential environmental harm.

The question is: how can every small farmer in Africa obtain a tailored fertilization plan based on their farm, their soil type, the nutrient requirements of the crop they want to cultivate, and their environmental conditions? This is exactly the gap MazaoHub closes — affordable soil testing at the farm, combined with AI-driven, crop-specific fertilizer prescriptions delivered through extension officers, so every dose is right for the soil and safe for the environment.

Share

Exploring the Distinctions: Precision Farming, Digital Farming, and Smart Farming

02 Jun, 2023  ·  Admin

Introduction

The agricultural industry is undergoing a remarkable transformation, driven by technological advancements. This shift has given rise to new concepts such as precision farming, digital farming, and smart farming. While these terms are often used interchangeably, they have nuanced differences. In this article, we delve into the distinctions, backed by case studies, examples, and research reports.

1. Precision farming (precision agriculture)

Precision farming and precision agriculture are commonly used interchangeably. They refer to an approach that leverages technology to manage farming operations based on the specific needs of individual fields and crops. The primary goal is to observe, measure, and analyze these specific needs to optimize agricultural production processes.

According to a report by McKinsey & Company (2016), precision agriculture is shaped by the convergence of two key trends: big-data and advanced-analytics capabilities, and the integration of robotics — including aerial imagery, sensors, and local weather forecasts. The implementation of precision agriculture techniques allows farmers to make data-driven decisions, leading to optimized resource usage, cost savings, and reduced environmental impact.

A research study conducted by Khakimov et al. (2020) in Denmark explored the impact of precision agriculture on winter wheat cultivation. The study found that techniques such as variable-rate nitrogen fertilization based on crop needs resulted in improved crop yield and reduced nitrogen losses, thereby enhancing environmental sustainability.

2. Smart farming

Smart farming involves the application of information and data technologies to optimize complex farming systems. Unlike precision farming, smart farming goes beyond precise measurement and focuses on the utilization of data to make informed decisions. It encompasses all farm operations and utilizes real-time data from various sources such as soil condition, climate, weather, and resource usage. By leveraging mobile devices, farmers can access real-time data on multiple parameters and rely on concrete data rather than intuition, leading to improved efficiency and productivity.

3. Digital farming

Digital farming brings these worlds together — combining precision agriculture and smart farming with end-to-end digital workflows, connecting every stakeholder from the farmer to the buyer. This is the philosophy behind MazaoHub: precision at the soil level, smart real-time decision-making, and a fully digital farm ERP that works even offline in the village — purpose-built for Africa's smallholder farmers.

Field Notes  /  Climate

Save My Grandmother: A Call on Climate Change

17 Aug, 2023  ·  Admin

Do not quench my hope, do not break my spirit. Do not pray for my downfall, for if only you knew where I came from, you would show me compassion. I have endured suffering enough to be here. There are siblings of mine who have perished due to poverty. Hold my hand. Join me to bring about change together.

The journey of the MazaoHub Climate Farm Management Software is a story of my grandmother, titled "Save My Grandmother."

I don't know what kind of family you grew up in, whether in Africa or beyond. My mother was a poor farmer, yet she managed to cover all my school fees through farming. She gained popularity in a village called Chanji, Sumbawanga, for brewing a local drink known as "Komoni" in Swahili — a mixture of maize porridge, processed and fermented for about three days or more. She sold this drink to pay for my education. Due to prolonged cooking over firewood, the smoke led to a lung disease that took her life.

I was left with my grandmother. My grandmother was always a farm person. We would go to the fields and dig for potatoes. The struggles of being a small-scale farmer without success are the hardships many African farmers continue to face even today. We farmed without agricultural guidance. Sometimes, when we asked for help from agricultural officers, they wouldn't come. We jokingly called our agricultural officer "Bibi Shamba" (Farm Lady) because she was a woman. She did what she could, but most of the time we worked on our own — and she was not wrong: how can one extension officer manage more than 3,000 smallholder farmers manually?

We mixed fertilizers without proper guidance. We cleared forests to make space for farming. Often, our crops dried up or got spoiled before they could mature. I can't forget the money we lost during those times. During the rainy season, we anticipated consistent rainfall, but sometimes it just stopped. All the corn we planted withered. Imagine if we had access to weather information.

Sometimes, we used the wrong fertilizers or the wrong seeds. Sometimes, even after harvesting, we were exploited by middlemen. I used to ride my bicycle for more than 90 kilometers to reach customers. Sometimes I carried maize on my head to sell, but found no buyers, and returned home on foot, having earned nothing. This is why I repeat: do not quench my hope, do not break my spirit, my friend. Do not wish ill upon me — join me to build the climate-smart farming infrastructure that my grandmother never had.

Share
